structured_choices.data_source: Electronic health records (EHRs) and retinal imaging from a large hospital network spanning 5 years (~10,000 patient records). Data is partitioned into three non-overlapping temporal cohorts: patients with baseline assessment and follow-up imaging at least 12 months apart, stratified by imaging facility and follow-up interval consistency.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary outcome: progression of diabetic retinopathy is defined as documented advancement to a more severe stage (ETDRS level or equivalent) on follow-up imaging 12–36 months after baseline, adjudicated by board-certified ophthalmologists blinded to baseline clinical data. Non-progression is absence of stage advancement over the same interval. Patients with incomplete imaging or follow-up <12 months are excluded.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with stratification by facility and follow-up interval. Training cohort: patients with baseline assessment and follow-up during months 1–36 of the dataset (approximately 5,500 patients). Validation cohort: patients with baseline in months 37–48 and follow-up by month 60 (approximately 2,000 patients). Test cohort: patients with baseline in months 49–60 with follow-up documented (approximately 2,500 patients). This temporal split prevents leakage of future clinical information into training. Each split is stratified by imaging facility (to balance facility-level quality variation) and follow-up interval length (to ensure the model generalizes across different follow-up durations). Justification: Diabetic retinopathy progression is inherently longitudinal; a temporal split respects the causal ordering and prevents the model from learning spurious associations with data collected after the outcome. Stratification ensures that facility-level confounds and follow-up variability are balanced across splits.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (scaling, normalization, imputation, imaging standardization) are fit exclusively on the training cohort. These fitted transformers are then applied identically to validation and test cohorts without refitting. This prevents information leakage from validation/test data into the preprocessing pipeline. Imaging standardization (e.g., histogram equalization, resolution normalization) is performed at the individual image level before feature extraction.
structured_choices.features: Multi-modal feature set: (1) Tabular EHR features: age at baseline, sex, diabetes duration, HbA1c (most recent value at baseline), systolic/diastolic blood pressure, serum lipid levels (LDL, HDL, triglycerides), eGFR, presence of diabetic nephropathy, current medications (ACE inhibitors, statins, insulin type/dose). (2) Imaging features: raw retinal images passed to the CNN (no hand-engineered features); the network learns convolutional representations directly. (3) Meta-features: imaging facility identifier, imaging modality (fundus photography type), follow-up interval in months, time since last imaging before baseline. No features constructed from the follow-up period or outcomes; all features are available at baseline or derived from baseline data only.
structured_choices.model: A multi-modal CNN architecture: a pre-trained ResNet-50 (ImageNet weights) processes retinal images; its penultimate layer (2048-dim) is concatenated with a fully connected embedding of tabular EHR features (64-dim, learned via a 3-layer MLP); the combined representation (2112-dim) is passed through two fully connected layers (512 → 256 → 128 neurons, ReLU, dropout 0.3) and a sigmoid output layer for binary classification. The model is trained end-to-end with Adam optimizer (learning rate 1e-4), binary cross-entropy loss with class-weight adjustment for imbalance, and early stopping on validation AUC (patience=10 epochs).
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression trained on tabular EHR features only (demographics, labs, medications; imaging is excluded). This baseline is given equivalent hyperparameter tuning: L2 regularization strength is tuned via grid search (C ∈ {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100}) on the validation set using the same class-weighting scheme as the CNN. The baseline uses the same train/val/test splits and preprocessing. Justification: This baseline represents the current clinical practice (risk scores derived from tabular data) and tests whether the addition of imaging information via deep learning provides genuine predictive advantage over traditional EHR-based assessment. By using only tabular features, the baseline is not handicapped by lack of imaging; rather, it establishes the contribution of CNN-extracted imaging representations.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area under the ROC curve (AUC) on the held-out test set. Justification: AUC is threshold-independent and reflects the model's ability to rank-order patients by progression risk—clinically meaningful because clinicians will set a threshold based on intervention capacity and risk tolerance. AUC is also robust to class imbalance (does not penalize a model that predicts mostly the majority class). The stakeholder's emphasis on reducing false negatives (identifying high-risk patients) is best addressed by examining the ROC curve and choosing an operating point (e.g., 90% sensitivity) that minimizes missed progressions; AUC captures overall discriminative ability across all thresholds.
secondary_metrics: Sensitivity (recall) at 90% specificity on the test set—operationally relevant because it reflects the proportion of true progressors correctly identified when the false-positive rate is constrained (limiting unnecessary interventions).
Precision-recall curve and F1-score (macro and micro) to assess performance on the minority (progression) class.

Calibration (Brier score, calibration plots) to ensure predicted probabilities are well-calibrated—important for clinical decision support, where miscalibrated confidence estimates could mislead clinicians.
Performance stratified by imaging facility and follow-up interval on the test set to assess whether the model generalizes across these sources of variation.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameters (ResNet-50 learning rate, dropout rate, number of epochs, L2 regularization, class weights) are selected via grid search + random search on the validation cohort. The metric used for selection is AUC on the validation set. The test set is never accessed during model selection. Early stopping monitors validation AUC and halts training if no improvement is observed for 10 epochs.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is held out and never used for any tuning, model selection, or exploratory analysis during development. Final performance metrics (AUC, sensitivity, calibration) are computed once on the test set at the conclusion of the experiment. No iteration or refinement is performed after test evaluation.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Facility-level imaging quality variation is controlled by stratifying the train/val/test splits by facility and by including facility identifier as a model input (so the model can learn facility-specific imaging characteristics). Follow-up interval variation is controlled by stratifying splits by follow-up interval length (e.g., 12–18 months vs. 19–36 months) and by including follow-up interval as a tabular feature in the model. After test evaluation, performance is examined separately for each facility and follow-up interval stratum to verify the model generalizes and does not degrade substantially in underrepresented groups. Patients with incomplete imaging (missing modality, corrupted images) or incomplete EHRs are excluded prior to split assignment, reducing missing-data confounds. The temporal split design ensures that progression outcomes are not predicted using contemporaneous or future clinical events, eliminating reverse causality.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: CNN-Based Multi-Modal Prediction of Diabetic Retinopathy Progression

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses electronic health records and retinal imaging from a large hospital network over 5 years, comprising approximately 10,000 patient records. We include only patients with a documented baseline assessment (EHR + imaging) and follow-up imaging at least 12 months later, ensuring sufficient longitudinal follow-up to observe progression. Progression is defined as advancement to a more severe ETDRS stage (or equivalent classification) on follow-up imaging, adjudicated by board-certified ophthalmologists blinded to baseline clinical data. Patients with incomplete imaging data (missing modality, corrupted files) or follow-up intervals shorter than 12 months are excluded before analysis.

Known data quality issues—variability in imaging facility standards and inconsistent follow-up timing—are documented and retained as stratification variables rather than removed. This ensures the model learns to generalize across realistic heterogeneity.

### 2. Split Strategy

We employ a **temporal split with stratification** to respect the longitudinal nature of the data and prevent information leakage. Patients are ordered by baseline assessment date and divided into three cohorts:
- **Training:** Baselines in months 1–36 (~5,500 patients)  
- **Validation:** Baselines in months 37–48 (~2,000 patients)  
- **Test:** Baselines in months 49–60 (~2,500 patients)  

Within each cohort, patients are stratified by **imaging facility** and **follow-up interval length** (e.g., 12–18 months vs. 19–36 months) to balance facility-level confounds and ensure the model sees similar distributions of follow-up timing across splits.

**Justification:** Diabetic retinopathy progression is inherently causal and temporal. A temporal split ensures that no future clinical information is available at the time of prediction, eliminating data leakage and respecting the chronological ordering of events. Stratification ensures that facility-level imaging variation and follow-up timing variation—both known confounds—are balanced across splits, allowing us to detect if the model degrades when deployed to new facilities or follow-up protocols.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All preprocessing steps (normalization, scaling, imputation) are fit on the training cohort only and then applied identically to validation and test sets. Retinal images undergo standardization (histogram equalization, resolution normalization to 512×512 pixels) before being fed to the CNN.

The multi-modal feature set includes:
- **Tabular EHR features:** Age, sex, diabetes duration, HbA1c, blood pressure, lipid panel, eGFR, presence of diabetic nephropathy, current medications (ACE inhibitors, statins, insulin).  
- **Imaging features:** Raw retinal fundus photographs (processed by the CNN; no hand-engineered image features).  
- **Meta-features:** Imaging facility, imaging modality type, follow-up interval in months.

**All features are derived from baseline data only.** No information from the follow-up period or outcomes is included, eliminating the risk of label leakage.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Proposed Model:** A multi-modal CNN combining a pre-trained ResNet-50 (ImageNet weights) on retinal images with a 3-layer MLP embedding of tabular EHR features. Image and tabular representations are concatenated and passed through two additional fully connected layers (512→256→128 neurons, ReLU, dropout 0.3) to a sigmoid output. The model is trained end-to-end with Adam (learning rate 1e-4), binary cross-entropy loss with class-weight adjustment for imbalance, and early stopping on validation AUC.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression trained exclusively on tabular EHR features (demographics, labs, medications; imaging excluded). This baseline represents current clinical practice and isolates the contribution of imaging-based deep learning. The baseline receives equivalent hyperparameter tuning (L2 regularization strength tuned via grid search on validation set) and the same class-weighting scheme.

**Justification:** The baseline is a fair comparison because it uses the same data splits, preprocessing, and evaluation conditions as the CNN. By restricting the baseline to tabular features, we test whether imaging adds genuine predictive value, not whether deep learning beats logistic regression due to increased model capacity on the same features.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC) on the held-out test set.**

AUC is chosen because:
- It is **threshold-independent**, reflecting the model's ability to rank-order patients by progression risk.  
- It is **robust to class imbalance**, the dataset's primary challenge.  
- It aligns with the stakeholder's operational goal: identifying patients at high risk enables clinicians to set a threshold (e.g., 90% sensitivity) that balances intervention capacity against missed cases.  

**Secondary metrics:**
- Sensitivity at 90% specificity (operationally relevant: proportion of true progressors identified when false-positive rate is constrained).  
- Precision-recall curve and F1-score (macro and micro) to assess minority-class performance.  
- Calibration (Brier score, calibration plots) to ensure predicted probabilities are reliable for clinical decision support.  
- **Stratified performance:** AUC, sensitivity, and calibration are computed separately for each imaging facility and follow-up interval stratum on the test set to verify generalization.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters (learning rate, dropout, batch size, class weights) are tuned via grid + random search on the **validation cohort** using AUC as the selection metric. Early stopping monitors validation AUC with a patience of 10 epochs. The **test set is never accessed** during development.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

**Facility variation** is controlled through stratified splits (each facility represented proportionally in train/val/test) and by including facility as a model input feature. **Follow-up interval variation** is similarly controlled through stratified splits and as a tabular feature. After test evaluation, performance is disaggregated by facility and follow-up interval to detect any model degradation in underrepresented groups. **Temporal ordering** is preserved by the chronological split, eliminating reverse causality. **Missing data** is handled by excluding incomplete records before splitting, reducing the risk of bias from imputation.
